title: The Day I Let Them Down

story.opening: <break time="400ms"/>Diego was seven when I missed his school play. <break time="350ms"/>His first one. <break time="300ms"/>I still think about it.
story.body: <break time="350ms"/>Luna was sick. <break time="300ms"/>Really sick. <break time="250ms"/>Couldn't leave her. <break time="400ms"/>My husband went to the play. <break time="350ms"/>Took video. <break time="300ms"/>But Diego looked for me in the audience. <break time="250ms"/>I watched it in the video later. <break time="200ms"/>His eyes scanning the seats. <break time="500ms"/>When I apologized, he said it was okay. <break time="400ms"/>Kids say that. <break time="350ms"/>But I saw his face when he came home. <break time="500ms"/>The guilt was physical. <break time="400ms"/>It lived in my chest for weeks.
story.reflection: <break time="500ms"/>Here's what I've learned. <break time="400ms"/>You can't be everywhere. <break time="350ms"/>You can't catch every moment. <break time="300ms"/>You will let them down sometimes. <break time="500ms"/>But they won't remember every missed play. <break time="400ms"/>They'll remember the pattern. <break time="350ms"/>The showing up, again and again. <break time="300ms"/>Even imperfectly. <break time="500ms"/>One missed play isn't a failed childhood. <break time="400ms"/>It's just one play.

when_to_share: When a parent is drowning in guilt over a specific parenting failure
